title: Credit Card Temptation
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: How can you use credit cards responsibly while avoiding the temptation to overspend?
What strategies can help you maintain awareness of your credit card spending in real-time?

stages.prompt: Credit cards feel like free money because:

options.label: They are income
options.reflection: Credit cards are not income - they're borrowed money that must be repaid. Thinking of them as income can lead to overspending and financial difficulties.

options.label: They provide unlimited spending power
options.reflection: Credit cards have spending limits and don't provide unlimited money. Exceeding your limit can result in fees and damage to your credit score.

options.label: They're a gift from the bank
options.reflection: Banks issue credit cards as a business service, not as gifts. They make money from interest charges and fees on your spending.

options.label: Payment is delayed but still required
options.reflection: Exactly! Credit cards create the illusion of free money because you don't pay immediately, but the payment is still required and often with interest.

stages.prompt: What is the main danger of credit card spending?

options.label: They're too convenient to use
options.reflection: While convenience is a factor, the real danger lies in the delayed payment structure that can hide the true cost of purchases.

options.label: The rewards programs encourage overspending
options.reflection: While rewards can be a factor, the fundamental issue is the psychological disconnect between spending and paying.

options.label: The real cost is hidden due to delayed payment
options.reflection: Perfect! When you don't pay immediately, it's easy to forget the actual cost and accumulate debt without realizing it.

options.label: They're less secure than cash
options.reflection: Credit cards generally offer better security than cash, including fraud protection and dispute resolution services.

stages.prompt: How should you treat credit card spending?

options.label: Spend freely since you'll pay later
options.reflection: Spending freely on credit cards can lead to accumulating debt that becomes difficult to manage. Always consider your ability to pay.

options.label: As part of your budget, not extra money
options.reflection: Exactly! Credit card spending should be planned within your budget, treating it as money you already have, not additional funds.

options.label: Only for emergencies and special occasions
options.reflection: While credit cards can be useful for emergencies, they can also be part of regular planned spending when used responsibly.

options.label: Ignore the balance until the bill arrives
options.reflection: Ignoring your balance until the bill arrives can lead to unpleasant surprises and potential overspending beyond your means.

stages.prompt: What happens when you only make minimum payments?

options.label: Most payment goes to interest, not principal
options.reflection: Exactly! Minimum payments are designed so that most of the money goes to interest charges, making it take much longer to pay off your debt.

options.label: Your debt remains manageable
options.reflection: Making only minimum payments typically means most of your payment goes to interest, causing your debt to persist and grow over time.

options.label: You benefit from the bank's grace period
options.reflection: The grace period only applies if you pay the full balance. Making minimum payments means you're already incurring interest charges.

options.label: Your credit score improves significantly
options.reflection: While making minimum payments is better than missing payments, it doesn't significantly improve your credit score and indicates high credit utilization.

stages.prompt: What is the best practice for credit card use?

options.label: Maximize rewards by using it for everything
options.reflection: Maximizing rewards by spending more than you can afford defeats the purpose and can lead to debt accumulation and financial stress.

options.label: Avoid credit cards entirely
options.reflection: While avoiding credit cards is one approach, responsible use can actually help build credit history and provide financial flexibility.

options.label: Carry a balance to build credit faster
options.reflection: Carrying a balance doesn't build credit faster and costs you money in interest. Responsible use without carrying balances is better for building credit.

options.label: Plan spending and pay the full balance monthly
options.reflection: Perfect! Planning your spending and paying the full balance each month allows you to enjoy the benefits of credit cards without the debt burden.
